note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses (never rewritten, summarized, or reordered; pronunciation spellings kept exactly as written in the script, e.g. 'four-oh-four', 'Logic P-O-S', 'Wash Central', 'C-R-M', 'hash mark', 'Jira'). Block windows are MEASURED onsets, not the nominal formula: Sigma(ffprobe per-file audio + 0.4) puts the s10 end at 211.177s, which overshoots the ffprobe-probed master (210.834s) by 0.343s (> 0.3 tolerance) because the concatenated MP3 frames decode ~0.02-0.34s shorter than the per-file duration sum. Onsets were therefore measured by FFT normalized cross-correlation of each WC-PB-14-sNN.mp3 (first 3 s template) against the joined master at 8 kHz mono PCM (all 10 blocks matched at score 1.000). Master ffprobe duration 210.834s.

phrases.text: Someone says a page is broken.
Before you believe them, or doubt them, reproduce it.
Load the exact address they used, then read two things:
the URL in the bar, and the message on the screen.
Three very different failures hide behind that one complaint,
and those two reads are what tell them apart.
Class one is the hard four-oh-four.
The Settings hub lists Logic P-O-S links,
and when they were last confirmed in July they served the web server's own error page.
No Wash Central header. No sidebar.
Just the server saying it has no file at that address.
Their status today is parked, so re-check on your own tenant before quoting it.
The same class wears a second face inside the app.
System Alerts still sits in the IT and Support menu,
yet on July ninth the page answered with an HTTP four-oh-four.
So a listed feature can still four-oh-four.
That usually means release gated, or mid deploy.
Never tell a client the feature doesn't exist.
Class two looks like nothing at all.
Fundraiser sits in the C-R-M sidebar like any other entry.
Click it, and watch the address bar.
The URL just gains a trailing hash mark, and the page never changes.
That is a stub. A menu item with no page wired behind it.
Not a four-oh-four, and not their connection.
Class three fools the most people.
Open Terminals, under Hardware.
The page loads.
Breadcrumb, KPI, buttons, column headers, all there.
But where the rows belong, it says it couldn't load records.
Failed to fetch.
Now look at what did render.
Header, sidebar, KPIs, columns. Only the data is missing.
The page shell arrived fine and one background call failed,
and that is usually transient.
On July ninth this same banner hit roughly sixty pages at once.
That's one service hiccup, not sixty broken screens.
Put the three side by side.
Whole page replaced by an error, at the address you asked for: hard four-oh-four.
A click that does nothing but add a hash mark: dead stub.
A page frame with a failed-records panel inside it: data-fetch error.
One look at the screen, plus the URL, settles it.
Whichever class it is, capture the same evidence.
The exact URL. A screenshot of the message.
The module you were in, where you clicked from, and what you expected to load.
Then the class-specific note: for a four-oh-four, whether it's a known parked item.
For a stub, say so. For a fetch error, whether a refresh clears it.
Then escalate from Support Tickets, under IT and Support.
On a real issue, on your tenant, the new ticket carries all five facts plus the class.
Two exceptions.
A fetch error that clears on refresh needs no ticket at all,
and migration switches go to Jira, not here.
On the shared demo, nothing gets submitted.
That's the sort.
Read the URL, read the message, pick the class.
Hard four-oh-four? Escalate it as a serving problem.
Dead stub? Log it as release gated, never as gone.
Fetch error? Refresh first, and escalate only if it sticks.
Three problems that look identical from a distance, told apart in one glance.
